metadata.title: What the Floor Hides
metadata.setting: A quiet English town in the present day, where an abandoned terrace house on Crane Street conceals a fifty-year-old secret.

characters.name: Jamie Cole
characters.description: A 20-year-old with dark hair and restless eyes who is quick to notice things other people overlook.
characters.flaw: Acts on impulse without thinking through the risk to himself or others, which consistently puts him in danger before he is ready.
characters.current_state: alert and uneasy, deliberately concealing the camera from Voss
key_items: old video camera (scratched casing, found wrapped in dark cloth behind the cupboard rag)

characters.name: Margaret Voss
characters.description: A 45-year-old estate agent with neat clothes and a businesslike manner who hired Jamie to clear the house quickly.
characters.flaw: Prioritises protecting her family's reputation above honesty, which makes her reckless when she feels cornered.
characters.current_state: businesslike and pressuring, unaware that Jamie has found the camera

characters.name: Mrs. Holt
characters.description: A sharp-eyed woman in her mid-seventies who has lived next door to the abandoned house for decades and notices everything.
characters.flaw: Stays silent when speaking out would help, because she fears the trouble that telling the truth might bring.
characters.current_state: watchful and still, silently observing Jamie from her front window

locations.name: The Abandoned House, Crane Street
locations.description: A narrow two-storey terrace house, empty for decades, its walls streaked with damp and its hallway thick with the smell of old plaster.

locations.name: The Front Room
locations.description: The largest room in the house, with a wide bay window facing the street, wide bare floorboards, and a single old cupboard set into the wall beside the fireplace.

locations.name: Voss Estate Agency
locations.description: A glass-fronted office on the high street, always tidy, with a framed photo of the town on the wall and a strong smell of fresh coffee.

locations.name: Mrs. Holt's House
locations.description: The terrace house immediately next door, its front window offering a direct view of the Crane Street property's entrance and front room.

episode_history.summary: Jamie begins clearing the abandoned house on Crane Street and finds an old video camera tucked behind a rag in the front-room cupboard, wrapped in dark cloth. The camera footage shows an unidentified middle-aged man hiding a rectangular object beneath a floorboard near the bay window. When Jamie investigates the same floorboard, he finds the dust wiped away and fresh scratch marks along its edge, showing it has been disturbed recently — someone else has already located the hiding spot but left without taking what is there. Jamie says nothing to Margaret Voss when she calls, and as he stands by the window he notices Mrs. Holt next door watching him with silent, unbroken attention.

unresolved_threads.description: Who is the unidentified middle-aged man on the camera footage, and what is the rectangular object he hid beneath the floorboard?
Someone recently disturbed the floorboard and located the hiding spot but left without taking what is there — who, and why did they stop?
Mrs. Holt watched Jamie with unusual, unmoving intensity — what does she know about the house or its history?
Jamie concealed the camera from Margaret Voss when she called — what will he do with it, and what happens if she finds out?

series_plan.central_question: What secret did the Warner family hide in that house fifty years ago, and who is willing to bury it again today?

arc_beats.beat: Jamie finds the old camera, watches the footage of the hiding, and discovers the floorboard has already been recently disturbed — someone else knows.
Jamie finds the metal box still hidden beneath the floor, and Mrs. Holt tells him who the family on the tape were — the Warners, who vanished from the street overnight in 1974.
Jamie opens the box and finds photographs and a handwritten note that point to a deliberate arson in 1974 — and the name on the note is Voss.
Margaret confronts Jamie, takes the box from him, and warns him to stop; Jamie is left with nothing physical — only the camera footage.
Jamie uses the camera footage and Mrs. Holt's testimony to force the truth into the open, facing direct intimidation from Margaret.
The truth about the 1974 arson and the Warner family's disappearance is exposed; consequences fall on those responsible, and Jamie's choice to act despite real risk is shown to matter.

from_targets.context: "The footage was grainy and the colour was washed out, but it was clear enough to follow."
"this board had been disturbed recently. Weeks ago at most, not the decades the rest of the house suggested."
"a line of fresh scratch marks ran from one end to the other, pale wood showing through clearly" / "a long scratch across the plastic, as if it had been dropped"
"a small object wrapped tightly in a piece of dark cloth" / "He unwrapped it carefully"
"He pulled the power bank from his bag and connected the camera to charge."
"something tucked behind the rag" / "Jamie tucked the camera under his arm"

additional_scaffolded_words.context: "The footage was grainy and the colour was washed out" — meaning: poor visual quality, not clear or sharp
"Her tone was brisk, the kind that did not expect to be argued with" — meaning: quick, efficient, and not inviting discussion
"a video camera, old but compact" — meaning: small and neatly contained

last_scene_position: Jamie is standing in the front room of the abandoned house (loc_002), beside the bay window, holding the old video camera under his arm. He has just crouched to examine the third floorboard from the wall — finding it wiped clean with fresh scratch marks along its edge — and straightened up to see Mrs. Holt watching him in complete stillness from the window of the house next door. The hidden object beneath the floorboard has not been touched, and Jamie has not yet decided what to do with the camera or the information it contains. Episode 2 begins after this moment of eye contact.
